note_data.title: Gravitational fields
keyPoints: A gravitational field is an area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
There are two types of gravitational fields: uniform fields (same force everywhere) and radial fields (force depends on position)
Gravitational field strength (g) is the force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field, calculated using g = F/m
Newton's law of gravitation states that gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
The gravitational force formula is F = Gm₁m₂/r² where G is the gravitational constant
For radial fields, gravitational field strength follows g = GM/r² showing an inverse square relationship
Mass of a uniform sphere acts as a point mass at its centre when calculating gravitational force on objects outside the sphere
Gravity always acts on objects with mass and is always attractive
Field lines show direction of force on a mass, with closer lines indicating stronger gravitational field strength

definitions.definition: An area in which an object experiences a non-contact force
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
Exerts the same gravitational force on a mass everywhere in the field
The force exerted depends on the position of the object in the field
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all algebraic steps clearly, starting from given equations
Demonstrate the calculation with all working and correct units

modelAnswer.question: A satellite of mass 500 kg orbits Earth at height 3.6 × 10⁷ m above the surface. Calculate the gravitational potential energy of the satellite. (Mass of Earth = 6.0 × 10²⁴ kg, radius of Earth = 6.4 × 10⁶ m)
modelAnswer.answer:
Distance from Earth's centre r = (6.4 × 10⁶) + (3.6 × 10⁷) = 4.24 × 10⁷ m
Using Ep = -GMm/r
Ep = -(6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 6.0 × 10²⁴ × 500)/(4.24 × 10⁷)
Ep = -4.7 × 10⁹ J
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct calculation of r from Earth's centre
Mark 3: Correct final answer with units

commonMistakes: Forgetting that gravitational field strength is constant in uniform fields but varies in radial fields
Not recognizing that uniform spheres act as point masses at their centres for external calculations
Confusing the direction of field lines - they show the direction force acts on a mass
Missing that gravitational force follows an inverse square relationship with distance
examinerTips: Remember that closer field lines indicate stronger gravitational field strength
Use the inverse square relationship: if distance increases by factor of 2, field strength decreases by factor of 4
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Radial field lines pointing towards Earth's centre, with equipotential circles (dotted lines) perpendicular to field lines. Show satellite in circular orbit with velocity vector tangential to orbit and gravitational force pointing towards centre.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why gravitational field strength varies with distance from Earth's center and calculate the field strength at different altitudes
N/D 2022 P1 Q12 (3 marks) — multiple choice question testing understanding of gravitational field strength on different planets with varying mass and radius
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — students had to sketch gravitational field lines around Earth and explain why satellites in geostationary orbit remain at fixed positions
N/D 2021 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — calculation question requiring students to find gravitational field strength on Mars given its mass and radius compared to Earth

workedExample.question: The gravitational field strength on Earth's surface is 9.8 N/kg. Calculate the gravitational field strength at a height of 6400 km above Earth's surface. (Earth's radius = 6400 km) [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Identify that gravitational field strength follows inverse square law: g ∝ 1/r²
Step 2: At surface, r = 6400 km. At height 6400 km, r = 6400 + 6400 = 12800 km = 2 × 6400 km
Step 3: Since distance doubled, field strength = g₀/(2²) = g₀/4
workedExample.answer: The gravitational field strength at height 6400 km is 2.45 N/kg
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for inverse square relationship, 1 mark for correct distance calculation, 1 mark for correct ratio, 1 mark for final answer with units

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused gravitational field strength with gravitational force' — always remember that field strength is force per unit mass (N/kg), not just force
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently drew field lines crossing each other' — gravitational field lines must never cross and should point toward the center of mass
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was not using radius from center when calculating field strength' — always measure distance from the center of the planet, not from the surface
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw Earth as a circle with equally-spaced radial arrows pointing inward toward the center, representing gravitational field lines. Show arrows getting further apart as distance increases to demonstrate weakening field strength. Label key features: 'gravitational field lines', 'direction toward center', 'spacing indicates field strength'. Include a small mass with weight vector showing direction of gravitational force.
note_data.quickRecap: Gravitational fields are regions where masses experience non-contact forces, either uniform or radial in nature. Field strength is calculated as force per unit mass and follows Newton's law of gravitation. The key relationship is the inverse square law, where gravitational effects decrease rapidly with distance.
